title: Teaching
description: The big ideas behind good teaching — each unpacked simply: what it is, its core concepts, and what it looks like in the classroom.

sections.title: Constructivism & Inquiry-Based Learning
sections.summary: Learners build understanding by doing — knowledge is constructed through experience, not delivered ready-made.

blocks.content: What it is

Constructivism holds that knowledge is built, not delivered. Learners actively make meaning by connecting new experiences to what they already understand, rather than absorbing information unchanged. It draws on Piaget's cognitive constructivism (individuals construct understanding through interaction with their world) and Vygotsky's social constructivism (understanding is built through language, culture and collaboration). Inquiry-based learning is its practical expression: students investigate genuine questions, test ideas, and arrive at understanding rather than being handed it.
Core concepts

items: Prior knowledge — new learning is built onto existing mental structures (schemas); what a student already knows shapes what they can take in.
Active construction — meaning is made through doing, questioning and revising, not through passive reception.
The Zone of Proximal Development (Vygotsky) — the space between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with support; teaching aims here.
Scaffolding — temporary, deliberate support that is gradually removed as competence grows.
Productive struggle (Piaget's disequilibrium) — the discomfort of not-yet-understanding is what drives genuine learning; resolving it builds durable knowledge.
Social construction — dialogue and collaboration are not extras; they are how much understanding gets built.

blocks.content: In the classroom

items: Inquiry units organised around a driving question students genuinely investigate
Problem-based and project-based learning anchored in real, messy tasks
Hands-on investigation — experiments, fieldwork, building and testing models
Hypothesise → test → reflect cycles, with students revising their thinking
Collaborative structures (think-pair-share, group investigation) that surface and challenge ideas
The teacher as facilitator and questioner rather than the sole source of answers

blocks.content: Where it meets AI

AI can accelerate the parts of inquiry that aren't the learning — gathering background, surfacing perspectives, getting unstuck — so students spend their effort on analysis and meaning-making. The risk is precise: constructivism requires that the student does the building, and AI is very good at building things.

items: Provide background quickly so cognitive effort goes to analysis, not hunting for basics
Generate multiple perspectives or sources for students to evaluate and reconcile
Act as a dialogue partner that helps students articulate and sharpen their own thinking
Serve as a research starting point — never the research conclusion

blocks.title: Skipping the construction
blocks.content: If AI produces the synthesis, explanation or conclusion the student was meant to construct, the learning does not transfer. The essay, the summary, the model is not the point — making it is. AI that shortcuts the process removes the pedagogy from the lesson, even when the product looks excellent.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Position AI as a resource to interrogate, not a source to accept. Ask students to question its outputs, find what's missing, and add what AI could not know from its own experience — the local, the lived, the just-happened.

sections.title: Cognitive Load Theory
sections.summary: Working memory is limited; learning means building lasting mental structures without overloading it.

blocks.content: What it is

Cognitive Load Theory, developed by John Sweller, starts from a hard limit: working memory can only hold a few new pieces of information at once, and only for a short time. Long-term memory, by contrast, is effectively unlimited and stores knowledge as organised structures called schemas. Learning is the process of building and automating those schemas. Good instruction therefore manages how much working memory a task demands, so that capacity is spent on understanding rather than wasted on confusion.
Core concepts

items: Working memory is severely limited — overload it and learning stalls; nothing reaches long-term memory.
Intrinsic load — the inherent difficulty of the material itself; managed through sequencing, not eliminated.
Extraneous load — effort wasted on poor design, clutter or distraction; this is the load to cut.
Germane load — the productive effort of actually building schemas; this is the load to protect.
Schema automation — once knowledge is well-built, it runs almost automatically, freeing working memory for new learning.
The worked-example effect — novices learn more from studying worked examples than from solving problems unaided.

blocks.content: In the classroom

items: Use worked examples before asking for independent practice, fading them as students gain fluency
Chunk material and introduce one new idea at a time rather than several at once
Strip decorative or redundant detail from slides and handouts (less is genuinely more)
Integrate diagrams with their labels rather than splitting attention across the page
Build in retrieval practice so core knowledge becomes automatic and frees capacity
Watch pacing — cognitive overload often looks like disengagement

blocks.content: Where it meets AI

AI is a powerful tool for managing load — both the students' and your own. It can generate clean worked examples, chunked explanations and retrieval questions in seconds, and it can take the extraneous load of admin and drafting off the teacher so attention is preserved for planning that matters. But AI's default output is often verbose and over-detailed, which adds extraneous load rather than removing it.

items: Generate step-by-step worked examples and pared-back explanations at the right grain size
Rewrite dense material to remove clutter while keeping the substance
Produce retrieval-practice and low-stakes quiz questions to automate core knowledge
Offload your own extraneous load (admin, formatting, first drafts) to protect planning attention

blocks.title: Fluency is not understanding
blocks.content: AI text reads smoothly, which can create an illusion of understanding for both teacher and student — it feels learned because it sounds polished. And if AI does the worked thinking, students never build the schema. Use AI to reduce noise, never to remove the desirable effort that actually constructs memory.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Ask AI to cut extraneous load (clutter, jargon, length), not germane load (the productive struggle of building understanding). Always trim its output before it reaches students — the first draft is usually too long.

sections.title: The Socratic Method & Dialogic Teaching
sections.summary: Understanding is drawn out through disciplined questioning and genuine dialogue.

blocks.content: What it is

The Socratic method uses disciplined questioning to expose assumptions, test reasoning and guide learners toward deeper understanding. Dialogic teaching extends this: learning happens through talk that is genuinely co-constructed, where ideas are built, challenged and refined between people. Both are fundamentally human practices — they require someone who can adapt in the moment, push back, sit with uncertainty, and follow a line of thinking wherever it leads.
Core concepts

items: Questioning over telling — understanding is drawn out, not poured in.
Surfacing assumptions — good questions make the unexamined visible so it can be tested.
Productive uncertainty (aporia) — the honest 'I'm not sure yet' that opens genuine inquiry.
Dialogue as thinking made public — talk lets reasoning be seen, examined and improved.
Building on others' ideas — accountable talk where contributions connect, not just accumulate.
Wait time — the silence after a question that lets real thinking happen.

blocks.content: In the classroom

items: Socratic seminars and discussion circles around a shared text or problem
Question stems that probe reasoning — 'How do you know?', 'What if…?', 'What's the assumption here?'
Deliberate wait time, and talk partners before whole-class responses
Structured debate and dialectic that require students to argue and counter-argue
The teacher genuinely withholding the answer to keep students reasoning
Norms for accountable talk — agreeing, disagreeing and building with evidence

blocks.content: Where it meets AI

AI can simulate Socratic dialogue — it will ask follow-up questions, probe assumptions and offer counterarguments on demand. That makes it a useful sparring partner and a rich source of material to critique. But it differs from real dialogue in ways that matter: it holds no genuine uncertainty, it cannot truly be persuaded, and it generates responses rather than reasoning toward them.

blocks.title: A technique that keeps the student thinking
blocks.content: Reverse the roles: instead of asking AI to explain something, have students explain it to the AI, then evaluate its response. Did it understand them? Does its follow-up question reveal a gap in their explanation? The student stays in the active, reasoning role — and AI becomes a mirror, not an oracle.

blocks.title: Borrowed authority
blocks.content: Because AI answers fluently and instantly, students can treat its output as settled truth and stop questioning. The Socratic move is to turn that instinct around: present AI text as a claim to be examined — 'What's missing? What assumption is this making? Where's the evidence?' — using AI to practise the very scrutiny it can otherwise erode.

sections.title: Differentiated Instruction
sections.summary: Tailoring how every learner reaches the same big ideas — varying the route, not the destination.

blocks.content: What it is

Differentiated instruction, most associated with Carol Ann Tomlinson, means proactively tailoring teaching to students' differing readiness, interests and learning profiles — so that every student can access genuinely challenging, worthwhile learning. It is not about giving some students less. It is about giving all students a route into the same big ideas, varying the path rather than the destination.
Core concepts

items: Differentiate content (what students engage with), process (how they make sense of it), and product (how they show what they know).
Respond to readiness, interest, and learning profile — not to fixed labels or 'ability' groups.
Respectful tasks — every version is engaging and worthwhile; no student gets busywork.
Same big ideas for all — the core concepts and high expectations stay constant.
Flexible grouping — groupings shift with the task and the data, never permanent tracks.
Ongoing formative assessment — differentiation is driven by what you keep learning about students.

blocks.content: In the classroom

items: Tiered tasks and levelled texts that reach the same learning intention by different routes
Choice boards and menus that let students pick how they engage or demonstrate learning
Scaffolds, graphic organisers and exemplars offered to those who need them
Varied product options — essay, presentation, model, recording — for the same outcome
Flexible grouping that reshuffles based on formative checks
Multiple entry points into a task so everyone starts somewhere productive

blocks.content: Where it meets AI

Differentiation has always been limited by one thing: time. Producing three versions of a text, tiered tasks and matched scaffolds for one lesson is hours of work, so it rarely happens consistently. This is exactly where AI changes the maths — it can generate levelled and tiered materials in minutes, making sustained differentiation practical at classroom scale for the first time.

items: Produce the same content at several reading levels, preserving the key ideas and vocabulary
Generate tiered tasks and scaffolds aimed at one shared learning intention
Create alternative explanations, analogies and worked examples for different entry points
Make EAL/D and home-language versions to widen access

blocks.title: Vary the access, not the expectations
blocks.content: The danger is using AI to quietly lower the demand for some students — shorter, easier, less. True differentiation keeps the thinking demand constant and varies only the access to it. And because differentiation is driven by individual children's needs, never enter student names or details into a consumer tool; describe the level you need, not the child. The judgement about who receives what stays with you.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Let AI absorb the production cost that made differentiation hard, and spend the time you save on the part only you can do: knowing your students well enough to match the right route to the right learner.

sections.title: Universal Design for Learning (UDL)
sections.summary: Designing for the full range of learners from the outset, rather than retrofitting accommodations later.

blocks.content: What it is

Universal Design for Learning (UDL), developed by CAST, is a framework for designing learning that works for the full range of learners from the outset — rather than building for an imagined 'average' student and retrofitting accommodations later. Its insight is that variability is the norm, not the exception, so flexibility should be planned in. UDL keeps goals firm but makes the means flexible, aiming to develop expert learners who are purposeful, resourceful and strategic.
Core concepts

items: Multiple means of Engagement — the 'why' of learning; offering different ways to spark and sustain motivation.
Multiple means of Representation — the 'what' of learning; presenting information in more than one format.
Multiple means of Action & Expression — the 'how' of learning; letting students demonstrate understanding in different ways.
Design for the margins — building for variability tends to help everyone, not only those at the edges.
Firm goals, flexible means — the learning outcome is fixed; the route to it is deliberately open.
Reduce barriers proactively — anticipate what gets in the way and design it out in advance.

blocks.content: In the classroom

items: Offer key content in more than one format — text, audio, video, visual
Provide choice in how students engage with a task and how they show what they know
Build scaffolds, glossaries and worked exemplars into the materials from the start
Allow varied modes of expression — written, spoken, visual, built
State clear learning goals, kept separate from the method of getting there
Plan accommodations as default options available to all, not special exceptions

blocks.content: Where it meets AI

AI is one of the most significant accessibility tools in recent memory, and it maps almost directly onto UDL's principle of multiple means of representation. On demand, it can transform content — adjusting reading level, translating, restructuring, converting to audio, generating alternative explanations — which makes proactive, inclusive design genuinely achievable in the time teachers actually have.

items: Rewrite a passage at a different reading level without losing the substance
Provide a student's home language alongside English
Generate alternative explanations using different analogies or examples
Produce structured outlines and described visuals for students who need them

blocks.title: Don't let AI become the student's voice
blocks.content: UDL's third principle — multiple means of expression — is where care is needed. If AI generates the writing, the explanation or the response, it has not expanded how a student can express understanding; it has replaced their expression with its own. Supporting expression means helping students find their own voice in their own way, not handing them a generated one.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Use AI proactively in planning to design access in from the start — not reactively as a last-minute accommodation. And remember that 'provided' or 'simplified' is not automatically 'accessible': check the output against the actual student in front of you.
